title: Conjecture 1.4 (Engbers)
statement_text: Fix $\delta \geq 1$ and any graph $H$. Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree at least $\delta$. There exists a constant $c(\delta, H)$ such that, for $n \geq c(\delta, H)$, we have $$\hom(G, H) \leq \max\left\{\hom(K_{\delta+1}, H)^{\frac{n}{\delta+1}},\, \hom(K_{\delta,\delta}, H)^{\frac{n}{2\delta}},\, \hom(K_{\delta,n-\delta}, H)\right\}.$$
context_text: Engbers studied which graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $\delta$ maximises $H$-colourings for large $n$. For $\delta = 1$ or $\delta = 2$ he showed the maximum is attained by one of $\frac{n}{\delta+1}K_{\delta+1}$, $\frac{n}{2\delta}K_{\delta,\delta}$, or $K_{\delta,n-\delta}$ depending on the structure of $H$, and conjectured the same trichotomy holds for all $\delta \geq 1$.
paper_contribution: Disproves Conjecture 1.4 for all $\delta \geq 3$ by constructing an infinite family of counterexamples using ideas similar to Sernau's refutation of Galvin's conjecture.
notes: PDF source — superscript/exponent notation garbled in extraction; statement reconstructed from context. Labeled 'Conjecture 1.4 [4]' in the paper with attribution to Engbers.

title: Engbers' Question: H-Colourings of Connected Graphs for All H
statement_text: For every $\delta \geq 3$ and every graph $H$, does $K_{\delta,n-\delta}$ uniquely maximise $\hom(G, H)$ among all connected graphs $G$ on $n$ vertices with minimum degree at least $\delta$, for $n$ sufficiently large?
context_text: Engbers [5] proved the result for non-regular $H$ and $\delta \geq 2$, and asked whether it extends to all graphs $H$. The paper says 'This answers a question posed by Engbers [5]' after presenting Corollary 1.2.
paper_contribution: Answers affirmatively via Theorem 1.1 and Corollary 1.2: for all $\delta \geq 3$, all graphs $H$, and $n \geq \kappa(\delta, H)$, the graph $K_{\delta,n-\delta}$ maximises $\hom(G,H)$ over all connected $G$ with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $\delta$.
notes: Question described in prose without a labelled environment; statement reconstructed from the paper's description of Engbers [5].

title: Engbers' Question: Maximising Proper q-Colourings Among Connected Graphs
statement_text: For fixed $\delta \geq 3$ and $q > 2$, does $K_{\delta,n-\delta}$ uniquely maximise the number of proper $q$-colourings among all connected graphs on $n$ vertices with minimum degree at least $\delta$, for $n$ sufficiently large?
context_text: Since proper $q$-colourings of $G$ correspond to $\hom(G, K_q)$ and $K_q$ is connected, this is a special instance of the general $H$-colouring question. The paper states this 'answers another question posed by Engbers [5]' after presenting Corollary 1.3.
paper_contribution: Answers affirmatively via Corollary 1.3, which follows immediately from Theorem 1.1 applied to $H = K_q$.
notes: Question described in prose without a labelled environment; statement reconstructed from context.

title: Engbers' Question: Maximising H-Colourings Among All Graphs with Minimum Degree δ
statement_text: For a fixed graph $H$ and $\delta \geq 1$, which graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with minimum degree at least $\delta$ maximises $\hom(G, H)$ for $n$ sufficiently large (without assuming connectivity of $G$)?
context_text: Engbers [4] asked which graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $\delta$ maximises $H$-colourings as $n$ grows, without a connectivity assumption. His Conjecture 1.4 proposed a specific answer; the abstract states Theorem 1.5 'partially answers another question of Engbers' in this direction.
paper_contribution: Partially answers the question via Theorem 1.5: for every graph $H$ with maximum degree $k$ that does not contain the complete looped graph on $k$ vertices or $K_{k,k}$ as a component, and for $\delta \geq \delta_0(H)$, the graph $K_{\delta,n-\delta}$ uniquely maximises $\hom(G,H)$ over all graphs $G$ on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $\delta$, for $n$ sufficiently large.
notes: Question described in prose without a labelled environment; statement reconstructed from the paper's description of Engbers [4]. The abstract explicitly states Theorem 1.5 partially answers this question.
